Being out here, working with my hands as a carpenter, you see things in real life—you see how things fit together, how they hold up, or when they’re just shoddy work. Talking about health online, it's like trying to build a house with mismatched boards; everything just rattles and feels unstable. When people start arguing about the science, or whether they should take it, my first instinct, because I’m so keyed up and I’m always looking for the next angle, is to dive right in, to find the ‘truth’ in the comments. But honestly, after a while, it just feels like a waste of good energy. If someone is just trying to bait me, or if they’re coming at me with facts that are totally twisted, the best thing I’ve learned, especially because I get so worked up easily, is to just pull back. I don't have the patience for endless, shouting matches, not when I’ve got work to do or a game of football to watch. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend hours online, and let me tell you, the noise about this whole vaccine thing is enough to drive a person crazy. Being out here, working with my hands as a carpenter, you see things in real life—you see how things fit together, how they hold up, or when they’re just shoddy work. Talking about health online, it's like trying to build a house with mismatched boards; everything just rattles and feels unstable. When people start arguing about the science, or whether they should take it, my first instinct, because I’m so keyed up and I’m always looking for the next angle, is to dive right in, to find the ‘truth’ in the comments. But honestly, after a while, it just feels like a waste of good energy. If someone is just trying to bait me, or if they’re coming at me with facts that are totally twisted, the best thing I’ve learned, especially because I get so worked up easily, is to just pull back. I don't have the patience for endless, shouting matches, not when I’ve got work to do or a game of football to watch. Instead of trying to win the argument, which is impossible when everyone is coming from a different corner, I try to steer it back to something more grounded. If I have to engage, I keep it simple, almost like checking a measurement twice. I'll say, 'Look, I hear what you’re saying, but for me, I’m trusting what the doctors I know say.' It’s not about being a coward; it’s about keeping my own head straight amidst all the digital chaos. If the discussion just turns into name-calling, I just mute the thread. My energy is better spent painting a good piece or just getting out on my bike to clear my head.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is highly extraverted and neurotic, spending many hours on social media, which suggests high engagement but also high emotional reactivity to conflict. As a carpenter with low openness, they prefer tangible, concrete realities over abstract debates. The advice must be direct, emotionally aware, and grounded in practical action (like disengaging). The tone should be highly energetic but slightly frayed due to high neuroticism. The focus is on managing the emotional toll of online arguments.
